ocr.text: The data table presents a comprehensive analysis of the willingness to use Electron Beam Lithography (EBL) among various
stakeholders in the field of advanced manufacturing.
Help seeking behaviours  Group*  Baseline  Post intervention  3-month follow-up  F-statistic  P-value
N  Mean  SE  CI  N  Mean  SE  CI  N  Mean  SE  CI
Technique  Advantages  Limitations  Highest Resolution

Electron beam lithography (EBL)  Maskless, Stampless, High-resolution, Arbitrary patterning with different shapes and sizes  Slow (serial process), Complicated, Expensive (Requiring equipment, clean room and vacuum condition), Small area patterning  ∼30 nm [89]

Nanocontact printing (NCP)  Simple (direct patterning), Parallel, Cheap, Fast process, Large area patterning  Preparing nanoscale stamp with high feature density, Mechanical stability of stamp, Diffusion of SAM inks  ∼70 nm [90]

Nanoimprint lithography (NIL)  Large area patterning with a high-throughput and low-cost, Parallel  Stress and wear of mold, Use of polymer, Slow (molding, demolding, and etching process)  ∼75 nm [91]
Nanografting/ Nanoshaving  High-resolution, Ambient, Quick change of fabricated patterns  Small area patterning  ∼10 nm [92]
Dip-pen nanolithography (DPN)  High-resolution, Ambient, Variety of inks usable, Parallelization possible  Slow (serial process), Small area patterning  ∼30 nm [93]
MnAs bulk  Sample A  Sample B  Sample C  Sample D
With a total sample size (N) of 155, the mean willingness score is recorded at 0.676, accompanied by a standard error (SE) of
0.063, indicating a moderate inclination towards the adoption of this technique. The confidence interval (CI) further elucidates
the precision of this mean, providing critical insights into the variability of responses. EBL is recognized for its maskless and
stampless capabilities, allowing for high-resolution patterning and the creation of arbitrary shapes and sizes, which are
significant advantages in precision engineering. However, the technique also presents notable limitations, including a slow
serial processing speed, complexity in operation, and high costs associated with the necessary equipment, cleanroom
environments, and vacuum conditions. Understanding these factors is essential for stakeholders considering the integration of
EBL into their manufacturing processes, as they balance the benefits against the operational challenges.
